quote: This is, this is Bucky with Ion. I'm reaching out about the solar for me for the online. How are you doing? Okay. Yeah.
stewart_read: The intro fails on three counts: the script is garbled ('solar for me for the online'), Bucky self-answers 'how are you doing' without pausing for Eleanor, and there is no legitimacy anchor — no Tesla framing, no form-fill reference that would tell Eleanor why this call is legitimate. The codex intro beat requires a clear company introduction, a reference to the form submission, and a human pause for response. None of the three land.
coaching_implication: Have Bucky drill the standard intro cold until it is clean and he pauses after 'how are you doing.' The garbled opener is a credibility leak in the first 15 seconds. Manager should play this clip and ask Bucky to recite the intro from memory — if he can't, that's the root cause.

quote: No. No. No. No. This is wide open sagebrush. There's no issue with solar here.
stewart_read: Eleanor volunteers that her rural property has zero shading or obstruction — she has already assessed her own solar suitability and is telling Bucky the site is clean. This is an unsolicited qualification signal. Bucky's response ('No. No. No. I'm not saying that there is') is defensive rather than anchoring on what Eleanor just handed him.
coaching_implication: This was the moment to anchor Eleanor's own words back to her: she already believes her property works for solar. A suggested rephrase: 'That's exactly what we like to hear — wide open exposure is ideal. Let me make sure we capture that for the design.' Instead Bucky defended himself against a concern Eleanor wasn't raising. Show Bucky this clip and ask: what did Eleanor just tell you about her property, and did you use it?

quote: Well, there's a home here. Maybe the map is old. I don't know. With your Google Maps doesn't find this.
stewart_read: Eleanor is telling Bucky she lives in a rural area where Google Maps doesn't resolve her address — a practical reality that explains the satellite-view gap. This is a trust-building opportunity: acknowledging the rural-property context would have signaled to Eleanor that Bucky understands her situation. Instead Bucky moves on without acknowledgment. The moment passes unused.
coaching_implication: A brief acknowledgment here — 'That makes sense, rural properties don't always show up cleanly on satellite' — costs nothing and signals competence. Bucky's silence reads as indifference. Coach him to name what the customer just told him before moving to the next question.

quote: Oh, well, then I don't qualify because that's under a 100.
stewart_read: Eleanor applies her own incorrect qualification threshold and begins to exit the call. Bucky's bare denial ('Oh, no. That doesn't mean you don't qualify') stops the bleed but provides no substance — no explanation of what the actual threshold is, no flip of the bill into emotional alignment, no anchor on what her bill pain actually represents. The codex requires capture AND flip; Bucky delivers neither.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have been intentionally brief to keep Eleanor moving rather than getting into a bill debate. Rejected — there is no subsequent move that uses the bill anchor. The brevity is reactive, not setup.
coaching_implication: After 'that doesn't mean you don't qualify,' Bucky needed one more sentence: what the actual threshold is and why her bill still matters to the design. Suggested intent (no fabricated numbers): 'The qualification is about your credit, not your bill size — and even a lower bill means we're designing a system that fits your actual usage, which is a good thing.' Manager should use this clip to drill the bill-anchor-plus-brief-explanation sequence.

quote: It's flat. Okay. I I wouldn't put it on the roof. I'm not putting anything on a flat roof.
stewart_read: Eleanor has already solved the flat-roof problem herself and is proposing a path forward (ground-mount). This is active engagement — she wants solar, she has thought through the installation constraint, and she is telling Bucky what she needs. The coaching point is not whether Ion offers ground-mount; it is that the setter should never make unilateral product-scope determinations. The escape hatch is the correct move regardless of what the answer turns out to be.
coaching_implication: Bucky needed the universal escape hatch here: 'That's a great question for our specialist — they handle installation configurations and can tell you exactly what's possible on your property. Let me get you on the calendar with them.' Whether Ion does ground-mount or not is irrelevant to the setter's job. Manager should use this clip to drill the escape hatch until it is automatic.

stewart_read: Bucky makes a unilateral product-scope determination that ends the call. This is the exact failure mode setter_scope_discipline is designed to prevent. Whether the claim is accurate is secondary — the setter's job is to bridge to a specialist for any installation-configuration question, not to close the door. Bucky's four words cost Ion the lead.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky knew with certainty that Ion does not offer ground-mount and was saving everyone's time. Rejected — even if accurate, the correct procedure is to bridge to a specialist who can confirm and potentially offer an alternative path, not to terminate the call unilaterally.
coaching_implication: This is the call-ending moment and it is entirely preventable. Manager should play this clip in the 1-on-1 and ask Bucky: 'What is your job when a customer asks about an installation configuration you're not sure about?' The answer is the escape hatch. Drill it until 'Oh, we don't do that' is replaced with 'Let me get you to the right person to answer that.'

quote: Oh, okay. Well, then it won't work. Well, thanks for getting back to me. You too. Bye bye.
stewart_read: Eleanor accepts Bucky's product-scope denial and terminates the call. This is the second self-disqualification in the call — the first (bill size) was stopped by Bucky; this one was caused by Bucky. The missed opportunity was bridging to a specialist for installation configuration, not routing to the roofing team. Eleanor's situation does not trigger cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned — she has a flat roof she does not want to use, not a roof she needs replaced.
coaching_implication: The call did not have to end here. Eleanor was qualified, engaged, and had proposed her own solution. The lead was lost to a four-word scope denial. Manager should flag this call as a recoverable lost lead and use it to anchor the setter_scope_discipline coaching conversation: the setter's job is to hold the lead and bridge, not to adjudicate product capability.

quote: is it you're paying a 150 a month on that electricity?
stewart_read: Bucky introduces a $150 figure that has no basis in anything Eleanor has said — she has not given a bill number yet. This is either a data-entry artifact from the lead record or an invented anchor. Either way, Eleanor immediately corrects him ('No. No. No. No. No.') and provides the real number ($75 high). Introducing an incorrect number before the customer has spoken undermines credibility and forces Eleanor into a corrective posture.
coaching_implication: If $150 came from the lead record, Bucky should have framed it as a question against the record: 'I have $150 here — is that about right?' If it was invented, that is a more serious coaching gap. Either way, the sequence should be open question first, then confirm against any record data. Manager should ask Bucky where the $150 came from.
